---
title: "Brighton & Hove: A Local Case Study"
subtitle: "Applying the multilevel attainment model to a single local authority"
author: "Adam Dennett with some assistance from Claude Code"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    theme: ucl-theme.scss
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 6
    fig-dpi: 150
    df-print: kable
    embed-resources: true
    include-in-header: header-logos.html
    include-after-body: footer-logos.html
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r setup}
#| label: setup

library(tidyverse)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(performance)
library(plotly)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(scales)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(jsonlite)
library(here)

source(here::here("R", "helpers.R"))

# ---- Load panel data and fit models ----
panel <- readRDS(here::here("data", "panel_data.rds"))

HIGHLIGHT_LA <- "Brighton and Hove"

# ---- Prepare model dataset (Analysis E: 9 predictors, all 4 years) ----
# panel_data.rds already contains carry-forward imputed workforce variables
# for 2024-25 (from 04_compute_derived.R), so we simply filter for complete
# cases — no need to bind_rows or separate years.

imputed_full_data <- panel %>%
  filter(MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school")) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0,
    PTFSM6CLA1A > 0, PERCTOT > 0, PNUMEAL > 0,
    !is.na(OFSTEDRATING_1), !is.na(gor_name), !is.na(LANAME)
  ) %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(remained_in_the_same_school),
    !is.na(teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent),
    average_number_of_days_taken > 0,
    !is.na(gorard_segregation)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    OFSTEDRATING_1 = factor(OFSTEDRATING_1,
                            levels = c("Outstanding", "Good",
                                       "Requires Improvement", "Inadequate"),
                            ordered = TRUE),
    gor_name   = factor(gor_name),
    LANAME     = factor(LANAME),
    year_label = factor(year_label)
  ) %>%
  droplevels()

contrasts(imputed_full_data$OFSTEDRATING_1) <-
  contr.treatment(levels(imputed_full_data$OFSTEDRATING_1))

# ---- Fit Analysis E models (imputed full panel) ----

# All Pupils
d_all <- imputed_full_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0) %>% droplevels()
contrasts(d_all$OFSTEDRATING_1) <- contr.treatment(levels(d_all$OFSTEDRATING_1))

mod_e_all <- lmer(
  log(ATT8SCR) ~
    log(PTFSM6CLA1A) + log(PERCTOT) + log(PNUMEAL) +
    PTPRIORLO + ADMPOL_PT + gorard_segregation +
    remained_in_the_same_school +
    teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent +
    log(average_number_of_days_taken) +
    (1 | year_label) + (1 | OFSTEDRATING_1) + (1 | gor_name/LANAME),
  data = d_all, REML = TRUE, na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 20000))
)

# Disadvantaged
d_dis <- imputed_full_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A), ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A > 0) %>% droplevels()
contrasts(d_dis$OFSTEDRATING_1) <- contr.treatment(levels(d_dis$OFSTEDRATING_1))

mod_e_disadv <- lmer(
  log(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A) ~
    log(PTFSM6CLA1A) + log(PERCTOT) + log(PNUMEAL) +
    PTPRIORLO + ADMPOL_PT + gorard_segregation +
    remained_in_the_same_school +
    teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent +
    log(average_number_of_days_taken) +
    (1 | year_label) + (1 | OFSTEDRATING_1) + (1 | gor_name/LANAME),
  data = d_dis, REML = TRUE, na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 20000))
)

# Non-Disadvantaged
d_non <- imputed_full_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A), ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A > 0) %>% droplevels()
contrasts(d_non$OFSTEDRATING_1) <- contr.treatment(levels(d_non$OFSTEDRATING_1))

mod_e_nondisadv <- lmer(
  log(ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A) ~
    log(PTFSM6CLA1A) + log(PERCTOT) + log(PNUMEAL) +
    PTPRIORLO + ADMPOL_PT + gorard_segregation +
    remained_in_the_same_school +
    teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent +
    log(average_number_of_days_taken) +
    (1 | year_label) + (1 | OFSTEDRATING_1) + (1 | gor_name/LANAME),
  data = d_non, REML = TRUE, na.action = na.exclude,
  control = lmerControl(optimizer = "bobyqa", optCtrl = list(maxfun = 20000))
)

# ---- Brighton & Hove data ----
bh_data <- panel %>%
  filter(LANAME == HIGHLIGHT_LA,
         MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school"))

# Add predictions from the imputed model
bh_data <- bh_data %>%
  mutate(
    predicted_all = predict(mod_e_all, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    predicted_dis = predict(mod_e_disadv, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE),
    predicted_non = predict(mod_e_nondisadv, newdata = bh_data, allow.new.levels = TRUE)
  )

# ---- Brighton & Hove: latest year per school ----
bh_latest_model <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  group_by(SCHNAME) %>%
  filter(year_label == max(year_label)) %>%
  ungroup()

# ---- Helper function ----
ordinal_suffix <- function(n) {
  suffix <- case_when(
    n %% 100 %in% c(11, 12, 13) ~ "th",
    n %% 10 == 1 ~ "st",
    n %% 10 == 2 ~ "nd",
    n %% 10 == 3 ~ "rd",
    TRUE ~ "th"
  )
  paste0(n, suffix)
}
```


# Introduction

This case study applies the findings of the multilevel Attainment 8 model to **Brighton & Hove**, examining how the national model's coefficients play out for a single local authority. Brighton & Hove is a useful test case because its secondary schools span a wide range of intake characteristics — from relatively affluent schools with low free school meals rates to schools serving communities with high levels of deprivation — all within one compact urban authority.

The analysis draws on the **imputed full panel model (Analysis E)**, which uses nine fixed-effect predictors with random intercepts for year, Ofsted rating, and local authority nested within region. For full details on the model specification and the code used to fit the `lmer()` models, see the [Model Experiments report](https://adamdennett.github.io/school_attainment_tool/model_experiments.html).

::: {.callout-note}
## Data note
The panel covers academic years 2021-22 to 2024-25. For 2024-25, four predictor variables (% low prior attainment, teacher retention, leadership pay %, and teacher sickness days) are carry-forward imputed from 2023-24 values. These observations are marked with triangular markers in the plots below.
:::


# The National Model {#sec-national-model}

The results presented in this case study are derived from a **multilevel linear model** (also known as a mixed-effects or hierarchical model) fitted to all mainstream secondary schools in England over four academic years (2021–22 to 2024–25). The model predicts **log-transformed Attainment 8** as a function of nine school-level characteristics, with random intercepts that allow the baseline to vary by year, Ofsted rating, and local authority (nested within region).

The model equation can be written as:

$$
\log(\text{ATT8}_{ij}) = \beta_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{9} \beta_k \, x_{kij} + u_{\text{year}} + u_{\text{Ofsted}} + u_{\text{region}} + u_{\text{LA|region}} + \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

where $i$ indexes schools, $j$ indexes school-year observations, the $\beta_k$ are fixed-effect coefficients, and the $u$ terms are random intercepts. Because the outcome is on the **log scale**, each coefficient represents the proportional change in Attainment 8 associated with a one-unit change in the predictor (or, for log-transformed predictors, an **elasticity** — the percentage change in ATT8 for a 1% change in the predictor).

@tbl-model-coefficients presents the full set of estimated coefficients from the All Pupils model.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Admissions policy dummy variables
`ADMPOL_PT` (admissions policy type) is a **categorical variable** with three levels: *"Non-selective in a highly selective area"* (i.e. a non-selective school in an area with grammar schools), *"Other non-selective"*, and *"Selective"* (grammar schools). Because it is categorical, `lmer()` automatically creates **two dummy (indicator) variables**, with *"Non-selective in a highly selective area"* as the **reference category** (its effect is absorbed into the intercept). Each dummy coefficient shows the difference in log(ATT8) relative to that reference group, all else being equal.
:::

```{r tbl-model-coefficients}
#| label: tbl-model-coefficients
#| tbl-cap: "Fixed-effect coefficients from the national multilevel model (Analysis E — All Pupils)"

library(kableExtra)

# Extract coefficient table
coef_tbl <- as.data.frame(summary(mod_e_all)$coefficients)
coef_tbl$Variable <- rownames(coef_tbl)

# Human-readable labels
var_labels <- c(
 "(Intercept)" = "Intercept",
 "log(PTFSM6CLA1A)" = "log(% FSM eligible)",
 "log(PERCTOT)" = "log(% overall absence)",
 "log(PNUMEAL)" = "log(% EAL)",
 "PTPRIORLO" = "% low prior attainment",
 "ADMPOL_PTOTHER NON SEL" = "Admissions: Other non-selective (ref: non-sel. in highly sel. area)",
 "ADMPOL_PTSEL" = "Admissions: Selective (ref: non-sel. in highly sel. area)",
 "gorard_segregation" = "Gorard segregation index",
 "remained_in_the_same_school" = "Teacher retention %",
 "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent" = "% teachers on leadership pay",
 "log(average_number_of_days_taken)" = "log(avg teacher sickness days)"
)

coef_tbl$Label <- var_labels[coef_tbl$Variable]
coef_tbl$Label[is.na(coef_tbl$Label)] <- coef_tbl$Variable[is.na(coef_tbl$Label)]

# Format for display
display_tbl <- coef_tbl %>%
  transmute(
    Variable = Label,
    Estimate = round(Estimate, 5),
    `Std. Error` = round(`Std. Error`, 5),
    `t value` = round(`t value`, 2),
    `Pr(>|t|)` = ifelse(`Pr(>|t|)` < 0.001, "< 0.001",
                         ifelse(`Pr(>|t|)` < 0.01, "< 0.01",
                                ifelse(`Pr(>|t|)` < 0.05, "< 0.05",
                                       round(`Pr(>|t|)`, 3))))
  )

kbl(display_tbl, format = "html", align = "lrrrr", escape = FALSE) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Reading the coefficients
The four log-transformed predictors (% FSM, % absence, % EAL, teacher sickness days) have coefficients that act as **elasticities**: a 1% increase in the predictor is associated with a $\beta$ % change in Attainment 8. The remaining continuous predictors enter on their raw scale, so their coefficients represent the proportional change in ATT8 for a one-unit increase. The two **admissions policy** rows are dummy variables for a three-level categorical predictor — each coefficient shows the difference relative to the reference category ("non-selective in a highly selective area"). In all cases, the effect on ATT8 in points depends on the school's starting ATT8 score — multiply the proportional change by the school's current ATT8 to get the point change.
:::


# Brighton & Hove's Position Among All Local Authorities

Before examining individual schools, it is important to understand where Brighton & Hove sits as a *local authority* in the national model. The caterpillar plot below displays the estimated random intercepts for each local authority (nested within region), ordered by magnitude. Error bars show 95% confidence intervals (±1.96 posterior SE). Local authorities whose interval does not cross zero are meaningfully different from the overall mean, after accounting for fixed effects and other random effects. Brighton and Hove is highlighted in pink.

```{r caterpillar-plot}
#| label: caterpillar-plot
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-cap: "Caterpillar plot of LA random intercepts (imputed full panel model, All Pupils). Error bars show 95% confidence intervals. Brighton and Hove is highlighted in pink"

library(ggrepel)

# Extract LA random effects with conditional variances
re_list_imp <- ranef(mod_e_all, condVar = TRUE)
re_la <- as.data.frame(re_list_imp$`LANAME:gor_name`)
re_la$group <- rownames(re_list_imp$`LANAME:gor_name`)

# Posterior standard errors
se_la <- sqrt(attr(re_list_imp$`LANAME:gor_name`, "postVar")[1, 1, ])
re_la$se <- se_la

re_la <- re_la %>%
  mutate(
    la_name = sub(":.*$", "", group),
    ci_lo = `(Intercept)` - 1.96 * se,
    ci_hi = `(Intercept)` + 1.96 * se,
    sig = ci_lo > 0 | ci_hi < 0,
    highlight = ifelse(grepl("Brighton", la_name), "Brighton and Hove", "Other")
  )

# Brighton's rank
bh_rank <- re_la %>%
  arrange(desc(`(Intercept)`)) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number()) %>%
  filter(grepl("Brighton", la_name))

n_las <- nrow(re_la)

# Top and bottom 10 for labelling (+ always include Brighton)
extreme_la <- c(
  head(re_la$la_name[order(re_la$`(Intercept)`)], 10),
  tail(re_la$la_name[order(re_la$`(Intercept)`)], 10)
)
if (any(grepl("Brighton", re_la$la_name))) {
  extreme_la <- unique(c(extreme_la,
                          re_la$la_name[grepl("Brighton", re_la$la_name)]))
}

ggplot(re_la, aes(x = reorder(group, `(Intercept)`), y = `(Intercept)`)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),
                width = 0, colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = highlight), size = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = re_la %>% filter(la_name %in% extreme_la),
    aes(label = la_name),
    size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 25, force = 4, nudge_y = 0.005,
    segment.colour = "grey60", segment.size = 0.3
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Other" = "#49a0c4",
                                  "Brighton and Hove" = "#e16fca"),
                      guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "LA Random Intercepts — Imputed Full Panel Model (All Pupils)",
       subtitle = "Ordered by estimate; error bars = 95% CI. LAs whose CI excludes 0 differ meaningfully from average.",
       x = "Local Authority (ordered by random intercept)",
       y = "Random Intercept (log scale)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

```{r caterpillar-interpretation}
#| label: caterpillar-interpretation

bh_intercept_val <- bh_rank$`(Intercept)`
bh_rank_n <- bh_rank$rank
bh_pct_effect <- round((exp(bh_intercept_val) - 1) * 100, 1)
bh_ci_lo <- round(bh_rank$ci_lo, 4)
bh_ci_hi <- round(bh_rank$ci_hi, 4)
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Brighton & Hove's random intercept

```{r bh-rank-inline}
#| output: asis
cat(sprintf("Brighton & Hove has the **%s highest** positive random intercept among all %d local authorities in England (estimate = %.4f on the log scale, 95%% CI: %.4f to %.4f).",
    ordinal_suffix(bh_rank_n), n_las, bh_intercept_val, bh_ci_lo, bh_ci_hi))
```

This means that, **after accounting for all nine model predictors** — intake deprivation, absence rates, prior attainment, workforce stability, segregation, and admissions policy — Brighton & Hove schools achieve attainment that is systematically higher than what the national model would predict based on their characteristics alone.

```{r bh-pct-inline}
#| output: asis
cat(sprintf("In practical terms, this translates to roughly **+%.1f%%** on the ATT8 scale — i.e. if the model predicts a score of 50 for a school with Brighton & Hove's characteristics, the LA effect alone pushes that prediction up to approximately %.1f.",
    bh_pct_effect, 50 * exp(bh_intercept_val)))
```

This is not a raw league table position. A high random intercept does not mean Brighton & Hove has the highest *raw* attainment — it means the authority is doing better *than expected* given the challenges its schools face. The confidence interval excludes zero, confirming this is a statistically meaningful effect.

Possible explanations for this positive "Brighton effect" ***could*** include a range of different factors: 

-   community factors not captured by the model (parental engagement, cultural attitudes to education, strong school-home ties)
-   consistently effective school leadership and governance across most of the schools in the authority.
-   local authority governance
-   school improvement programmes
-   effective school-to-school collaboration networks

While all of these are examples of local authority or school-level factors that could account for the residual variance not captured by the model, new data or additional qualitative work would be required to establish potential causal pathways. 

It is also worth noting that we have kept the number of explanatory variables as few as possible and selected just those which have most effect and contribute most to the story. In other associated files on this page, we experimented with other financial variables and those associated with special educational needs. While both added a very small amount of additional explanatory power, most of the their effect was bound up in other variables so we left out in the name of parsimony. Including them will have added an additional 1-2% of explanatory power. 
:::


# Brighton & Hove Schools Map

```{r bh-map}
#| label: bh-map
#| fig-cap: "Brighton & Hove secondary schools coloured by most recent Attainment 8 score (All Pupils)"

# Get latest year data for each school
bh_latest <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  group_by(URN) %>%
  filter(year_label == max(year_label)) %>%
  ungroup()

# Convert easting/northing to lat/long for leaflet
if (all(c("easting", "northing") %in% names(bh_latest))) {
  bh_sf <- bh_latest %>%
    filter(!is.na(easting), !is.na(northing)) %>%
    st_as_sf(coords = c("easting", "northing"), crs = 27700) %>%
    st_transform(4326)

  coords <- st_coordinates(bh_sf)
  bh_sf$lon <- coords[, 1]
  bh_sf$lat <- coords[, 2]

  # Colour palette based on ATT8
  att8_range <- range(bh_sf$ATT8SCR, na.rm = TRUE)
  pal <- colorNumeric(
    palette = c("#7b132b", "#ff7f89", "#f9dd73", "#abc766", "#2e6260"),
    domain = att8_range
  )

  # Scale radius by ATT8 score (rescale to sensible pixel range)
  radius_range <- c(6, 18)
  bh_sf$att8_radius <- scales::rescale(bh_sf$ATT8SCR,
                                        to = radius_range,
                                        from = att8_range)

  leaflet(bh_sf, width = "100%", height = 500) %>%
    addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) %>%
    addCircleMarkers(
      lng = ~lon, lat = ~lat,
      radius = ~att8_radius,
      fillColor = ~pal(ATT8SCR),
      fillOpacity = 0.85,
      color = "white",
      weight = 1.5,
      popup = ~paste0(
        "<b>", SCHNAME, "</b><br>",
        "Year: ", year_label, "<br>",
        "ATT8 (All): ", round(ATT8SCR, 1), "<br>",
        "ATT8 (Disadv.): ", round(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A, 1), "<br>",
        "ATT8 (Non-Dis.): ", round(ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A, 1), "<br>",
        "% FSM: ", round(PTFSM6CLA1A, 1), "%<br>",
        "% Absence: ", round(PERCTOT, 1), "%<br>",
        "Ofsted: ", OFSTEDRATING_1
      )
    ) %>%
    addLegend(
      position = "bottomright",
      pal = pal,
      values = ~ATT8SCR,
      title = "ATT8 Score<br>(size = score)",
      opacity = 0.85
    )
} else {
  cat("Coordinate data not available for mapping.")
}
```

The map above shows the location of each Brighton & Hove secondary school, coloured by its most recent Attainment 8 score. Click on any marker to see the school's key characteristics. The spatial pattern reveals how attainment varies across the city, with notable differences between schools in the more affluent west and the more deprived east.


# School Profiles

```{r bh-profiles}
#| label: bh-profiles

bh_profiles <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  group_by(School = SCHNAME) %>%
  summarise(
    Years = n(),
    `Mean ATT8 (All)` = round(mean(ATT8SCR, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Mean ATT8 (Disadv.)` = round(mean(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Mean ATT8 (Non-Dis.)` = round(mean(ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Mean % FSM` = round(mean(PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Mean % Absence` = round(mean(PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Mean % EAL` = round(mean(PNUMEAL, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(`Mean ATT8 (All)`))

knitr::kable(bh_profiles,
             caption = "Brighton & Hove secondary schools: average characteristics across available years")
```


# School Characteristics Map

Use the dropdown below to explore how different outcome and predictor
variables are distributed across Brighton & Hove's secondary schools.
Circle colour and size both reflect the selected variable.

```{r bh-map-interactive}
#| label: bh-map-interactive
#| fig-cap: "Brighton & Hove secondary schools — select a variable to change colour and circle size"

if (exists("bh_sf")) {

  # --- 1. Variable metadata ---
  var_meta <- list(
    list(var = "PTFSM6CLA1A", label = "% FSM Eligible", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "PERCTOT", label = "% Overall Absence", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "PNUMEAL", label = "% EAL", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "PTPRIORLO", label = "% Low Prior Attainment", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "gorard_segregation", label = "Gorard Segregation Index", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "remained_in_the_same_school", label = "Teacher Retention (%)", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent", label = "% Leadership Pay", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "average_number_of_days_taken", label = "Avg Teacher Sickness Days", group = "Predictor"),
    list(var = "ATT8SCR", label = "Attainment 8 (All Pupils)", group = "Outcome"),
    list(var = "ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A", label = "Attainment 8 (Disadvantaged)", group = "Outcome"),
    list(var = "ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A", label = "Attainment 8 (Non-Disadv.)", group = "Outcome")
  )
  var_json <- toJSON(var_meta, auto_unbox = TRUE)

  # --- 2. Serialise school data to JSON ---
  school_data <- bh_sf %>%
    sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
    select(SCHNAME, OFSTEDRATING_1, year_label, lon, lat,
           ATT8SCR, ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A, ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A,
           PTFSM6CLA1A, PERCTOT, PNUMEAL, PTPRIORLO,
           gorard_segregation, remained_in_the_same_school,
           teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent,
           average_number_of_days_taken) %>%
    mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ ifelse(is.finite(.), ., NA_real_)))
  data_json <- toJSON(school_data, na = "null")

  # --- 3. Bare leaflet (JS will add markers) ---
  m <- leaflet(width = "100%", height = 500) %>%
    addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) %>%
    setView(lng = mean(bh_sf$lon, na.rm = TRUE),
            lat = mean(bh_sf$lat, na.rm = TRUE), zoom = 13)

  # --- 4. Hidden <script> tag with data ---
  data_tag <- htmltools::tags$script(
    type = "application/json",
    id   = "school-char-data",
    htmltools::HTML(as.character(data_json))
  )

  # --- 5. onRender JS ---
  js_code <- paste0('
function(el, x) {
  var map = this;
  var varOpts = ', var_json, ';
  var schools = JSON.parse(
    document.getElementById("school-char-data").textContent
  );
  var defaultVar = "PTFSM6CLA1A";

  // CASA colour ramp: #bbd2f0 -> #2e6260 -> #4e3c56
  var C = [[187,210,240],[46,98,96],[78,60,86]];
  function colScale(t) {
    t = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, t));
    var i = Math.min(Math.floor(t * 2), 1);
    var f = t * 2 - i;
    return "rgb(" +
      Math.round(C[i][0] + f*(C[i+1][0]-C[i][0])) + "," +
      Math.round(C[i][1] + f*(C[i+1][1]-C[i][1])) + "," +
      Math.round(C[i][2] + f*(C[i+1][2]-C[i][2])) + ")";
  }

  var markers = L.layerGroup().addTo(map);

  // Legend
  var legCtrl = L.control({position: "bottomright"});
  var legDiv;
  legCtrl.onAdd = function() {
    legDiv = L.DomUtil.create("div");
    legDiv.style.cssText = "background:white;padding:8px 12px;" +
      "border-radius:5px;box-shadow:0 1px 5px rgba(0,0,0,.25);" +
      "font-size:12px;line-height:1.6;max-width:170px;";
    return legDiv;
  };
  legCtrl.addTo(map);

  function setLegend(label, lo, hi, n) {
    if (!legDiv) return;
    legDiv.innerHTML =
      "<strong>" + label + "</strong><br>" +
      "<div style=\\"height:12px;border-radius:2px;margin:4px 0;" +
      "background:linear-gradient(to right,rgb(187,210,240),rgb(46,98,96),rgb(78,60,86))" +
      "\\"></div>" +
      "<span>" + lo.toFixed(1) + "</span>" +
      "<span style=\\"float:right\\">" + hi.toFixed(1) + "</span><br>" +
      "<em style=\\"font-size:11px;color:#666\\">Showing " + n + " schools</em>";
  }

  function popup(d) {
    var f = function(v) { return v != null ? (+v).toFixed(1) : "N/A"; };
    return "<b>" + d.SCHNAME + "</b><br>" +
      "Year: " + d.year_label + "<br>" +
      "ATT8 (All): " + f(d.ATT8SCR) + "<br>" +
      "ATT8 (Disadv.): " + f(d.ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A) + "<br>" +
      "ATT8 (Non-Dis.): " + f(d.ATT8SCR_NFSM6CLA1A) + "<br>" +
      "% FSM: " + f(d.PTFSM6CLA1A) + "%<br>" +
      "% Absence: " + f(d.PERCTOT) + "%<br>" +
      "% EAL: " + f(d.PNUMEAL) + "%<br>" +
      "% Low Prior: " + f(d.PTPRIORLO) + "%<br>" +
      "Segregation: " + f(d.gorard_segregation) + "<br>" +
      "Teacher Retention: " + f(d.remained_in_the_same_school) + "%<br>" +
      "% Leadership Pay: " + f(d.teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent) + "%<br>" +
      "Sickness Days: " + f(d.average_number_of_days_taken) + "<br>" +
      "Ofsted: " + (d.OFSTEDRATING_1 || "N/A");
  }

  function draw(varName) {
    markers.clearLayers();
    var meta = varOpts.find(function(v){return v["var"]===varName;});
    var label = meta ? meta.label : varName;
    var vals = schools.map(function(d){return d[varName];})
                      .filter(function(v){return v!=null && !isNaN(v);});
    if (vals.length === 0) { setLegend(label, 0, 0, 0); return; }
    var lo = Math.min.apply(null, vals);
    var hi = Math.max.apply(null, vals);
    var rng = (hi - lo) || 1;
    var n = 0;
    schools.forEach(function(d) {
      var v = d[varName];
      if (v == null || isNaN(v) || d.lat == null) return;
      var t = (v - lo) / rng;
      L.circleMarker([d.lat, d.lon], {
        radius: 6 + t * 12,
        fillColor: colScale(t),
        fillOpacity: 0.85,
        color: "white",
        weight: 1.5
      }).bindPopup(popup(d)).addTo(markers);
      n++;
    });
    setLegend(label, lo, hi, n);
  }

  // Dropdown
  var wrap = document.createElement("div");
  wrap.style.cssText = "margin-bottom:10px;font-size:14px;";
  var lbl = document.createElement("label");
  lbl.textContent = "Colour & size by: ";
  lbl.style.fontWeight = "bold";
  wrap.appendChild(lbl);

  var sel = document.createElement("select");
  sel.style.cssText = "padding:4px 8px;font-size:13px;min-width:260px;" +
    "border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:4px;";
  var curGrp = "";
  varOpts.forEach(function(v) {
    if (v.group !== curGrp) {
      var og = document.createElement("optgroup");
      og.label = v.group + " Variables";
      sel.appendChild(og);
      curGrp = v.group;
    }
    var opt = document.createElement("option");
    opt.value = v["var"];
    opt.textContent = v.label;
    if (v["var"] === defaultVar) opt.selected = true;
    sel.querySelector("optgroup:last-child").appendChild(opt);
  });
  wrap.appendChild(sel);
  el.parentNode.insertBefore(wrap, el);

  sel.addEventListener("change", function() { draw(sel.value); });
  draw(defaultVar);
}
')

  m <- m %>% onRender(js_code)

  # --- 6. Combine data tag + map ---
  htmltools::browsable(htmltools::tagList(data_tag, m))
}
```

Comparing the two maps reveals the geographic correlation between intake deprivation and attainment in Brighton & Hove. Use the dropdown to explore how different variables relate to school location — for instance, switching between % FSM and Attainment 8 shows how schools with higher deprivation in the eastern parts of the city tend to have lower attainment, while schools in the west show the opposite pattern.


# Non-Linear Effects of Log-Transformed Predictors {#sec-nonlinear}

A key feature of the multilevel model is that four predictors enter on the **log scale**: % FSM, % absence, % EAL, and average teacher sickness days. This means the relationship between these predictors and attainment is **non-linear** on the original (raw) scale — a given absolute change in a predictor has a *different* predicted effect depending on where the school starts.

Before exploring these non-linear patterns, it is helpful to see how the model produces a prediction for a single school. The worked example below uses **Longhill High School** to show how each variable contributes to the predicted Attainment 8 score, and how changes in key variables shift that prediction.

## Worked example: Longhill High School {#sec-worked-example}

```{r longhill-worked-example}
#| label: longhill-worked-example
#| output: asis

library(kableExtra)

# ---- Extract Longhill's data ----
longhill <- bh_latest_model %>% filter(grepl("Longhill", SCHNAME))

if (nrow(longhill) == 0) {
  cat("Longhill High School not found in the data.\n")
} else {
  longhill <- longhill[1, ]

  # ---- Use predict() as the ground truth ----
  pred_check <- predict(mod_e_all, newdata = longhill, allow.new.levels = TRUE)
  pred_att8 <- exp(pred_check)
  actual_att8 <- longhill$ATT8SCR
  residual <- actual_att8 - pred_att8

  # ---- Fixed effects ----
  fe <- fixef(mod_e_all)

  # ---- Build the predictor contribution table ----
  # Each row: human label, raw value, model input value, coefficient, contribution
  # ADMPOL_PT is categorical → dummy-coded. Reference = "NON SEL IN HIGHLY SEL AREA"
  # Longhill is "OTHER NON SEL" so the dummy ADMPOL_PTOTHER NON SEL = 1

  admpol_val <- as.character(longhill$ADMPOL_PT)
  admpol_dummy_name <- paste0("ADMPOL_PT", admpol_val)
  admpol_beta <- if (admpol_dummy_name %in% names(fe)) unname(fe[admpol_dummy_name]) else 0
  admpol_contrib <- admpol_beta  # dummy = 1 for this category

  pred_rows <- list(
    list("Intercept",                     "—",    1,                                      unname(fe["(Intercept)"]),                                unname(fe["(Intercept)"])),
    list("% FSM eligible",                round(longhill$PTFSM6CLA1A, 1),                 round(log(longhill$PTFSM6CLA1A), 4),                      unname(fe["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"]),                           unname(fe["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"]) * log(longhill$PTFSM6CLA1A)),
    list("% overall absence",             round(longhill$PERCTOT, 1),                      round(log(longhill$PERCTOT), 4),                          unname(fe["log(PERCTOT)"]),                                unname(fe["log(PERCTOT)"]) * log(longhill$PERCTOT)),
    list("% EAL",                         round(longhill$PNUMEAL, 1),                      round(log(longhill$PNUMEAL), 4),                          unname(fe["log(PNUMEAL)"]),                                unname(fe["log(PNUMEAL)"]) * log(longhill$PNUMEAL)),
    list("% low prior attainment",        round(longhill$PTPRIORLO, 1),                    round(longhill$PTPRIORLO, 4),                             unname(fe["PTPRIORLO"]),                                   unname(fe["PTPRIORLO"]) * longhill$PTPRIORLO),
    list(paste0("Admissions policy (", admpol_val, ")"), admpol_val, 1,                    round(admpol_beta, 5),                                    admpol_contrib),
    list("Gorard segregation index",      round(longhill$gorard_segregation, 3),            round(longhill$gorard_segregation, 4),                    unname(fe["gorard_segregation"]),                          unname(fe["gorard_segregation"]) * longhill$gorard_segregation),
    list("Teacher retention %",           round(longhill$remained_in_the_same_school, 1),   round(longhill$remained_in_the_same_school, 4),           unname(fe["remained_in_the_same_school"]),                  unname(fe["remained_in_the_same_school"]) * longhill$remained_in_the_same_school),
    list("% on leadership pay",           round(longhill$teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent, 1), round(longhill$teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent, 4), unname(fe["teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent"]), unname(fe["teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent"]) * longhill$teachers_on_leadership_pay_range_percent),
    list("Avg teacher sickness days",     round(longhill$average_number_of_days_taken, 1),  round(log(longhill$average_number_of_days_taken), 4),     unname(fe["log(average_number_of_days_taken)"]),           unname(fe["log(average_number_of_days_taken)"]) * log(longhill$average_number_of_days_taken))
  )

  contrib_tbl <- data.frame(
    Variable = sapply(pred_rows, `[[`, 1),
    Raw_value = as.character(sapply(pred_rows, `[[`, 2)),
    Model_input = as.character(sapply(pred_rows, function(x) if (is.numeric(x[[3]])) round(x[[3]], 4) else x[[3]])),
    Coefficient = round(sapply(pred_rows, `[[`, 4), 5),
    Contribution = round(sapply(pred_rows, `[[`, 5), 4),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  names(contrib_tbl) <- c("Variable", "Raw value", "Model input (x)", "Coefficient (\u03b2)", "\u03b2 \u00d7 x")

  # ---- Random effects ----
  re <- ranef(mod_e_all)

  yr <- as.character(longhill$year_label)
  ofsted <- as.character(longhill$OFSTEDRATING_1)
  la <- as.character(longhill$LANAME)
  region <- as.character(longhill$gor_name)
  la_region <- paste0(la, ":", region)

  re_year <- if (yr %in% rownames(re$year_label)) re$year_label[yr, 1] else 0
  re_ofsted <- if (ofsted %in% rownames(re$OFSTEDRATING_1)) re$OFSTEDRATING_1[ofsted, 1] else 0
  re_region <- if (region %in% rownames(re$gor_name)) re$gor_name[region, 1] else 0
  re_la <- if (la_region %in% rownames(re[["LANAME:gor_name"]])) re[["LANAME:gor_name"]][la_region, 1] else 0

  fixed_sum <- sum(contrib_tbl[["\u03b2 \u00d7 x"]])
  random_sum <- re_year + re_ofsted + re_region + re_la
  manual_pred_log <- fixed_sum + random_sum

  cat(sprintf("### Step 1: Longhill's predictor values and their contributions\n\n"))
  cat(sprintf("The table below shows each variable in the model, Longhill High School's value for the most recent year (%s), how that value enters the model (log-transformed where applicable), the national model coefficient, and the resulting contribution to predicted log(ATT8).\n\n", yr))

  print(kbl(contrib_tbl, format = "html", align = "lrrrc", escape = FALSE) %>%
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                  full_width = FALSE) %>%
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
    row_spec(1, italic = TRUE))

  cat(sprintf("\n\n**Sum of fixed-effect contributions:** %.4f\n\n", fixed_sum))

  cat(sprintf("### Step 2: Adding the random effects\n\n"))
  cat("The multilevel model also includes random intercepts that adjust the baseline for contextual groupings:\n\n")

  re_tbl <- data.frame(
    Group = c(
      sprintf("Year (%s)", yr),
      sprintf("Ofsted rating (%s)", ofsted),
      sprintf("Region (%s)", region),
      sprintf("LA (%s)", la)
    ),
    Adjustment = round(c(re_year, re_ofsted, re_region, re_la), 5),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  names(re_tbl) <- c("Random effect", "Adjustment")

  print(kbl(re_tbl, format = "html", align = "lr", escape = FALSE) %>%
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                  full_width = FALSE) %>%
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE))

  cat(sprintf("\n\n**Sum of random effects:** %.4f\n\n", random_sum))

  cat(sprintf("### Step 3: The prediction\n\n"))
  cat(sprintf("Adding the fixed and random components:\n\n"))
  cat(sprintf("$$\\log(\\widehat{\\text{ATT8}}) = %.4f + %.4f = %.4f$$\n\n", fixed_sum, random_sum, manual_pred_log))
  cat(sprintf("$$\\widehat{\\text{ATT8}} = e^{%.4f} = %.1f$$\n\n", manual_pred_log, exp(manual_pred_log)))
  cat(sprintf("Longhill's **actual Attainment 8** in %s was **%.1f**, so the residual (actual − predicted) is **%+.1f** points.\n\n", yr, actual_att8, residual))
  cat(sprintf("*(Internal check: `predict()` returns %.4f on the log scale = %.1f on the ATT8 scale.)*\n\n", pred_check, pred_att8))
}
```

```{r longhill-scenarios}
#| label: longhill-scenarios
#| output: asis

if (exists("longhill") && nrow(longhill) > 0) {
  fe <- fixef(mod_e_all)
  beta_fsm_wk <- unname(fe["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"])
  beta_abs_wk <- unname(fe["log(PERCTOT)"])

  # City average FSM and national average absence (for realistic scenarios)
  city_avg_fsm_wk <- mean(bh_latest_model$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)

  national_latest <- panel %>%
    filter(MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school"), !is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
    group_by(SCHNAME) %>%
    filter(year_label == max(year_label)) %>%
    ungroup()
  national_avg_abs_wk <- mean(national_latest$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)

  cat("### Step 4: What happens when a variable changes?\n\n")
  cat("The power of the model is that we can change one variable at a time and see how the prediction shifts. Below we show two realistic scenarios for Longhill, each corresponding to a plausible policy target.\n\n")

  # ---- Scenario A: FSM reduces to city average ----
  current_fsm <- longhill$PTFSM6CLA1A

  if (current_fsm > city_avg_fsm_wk) {
    cat(sprintf("#### Scenario A: %% FSM falls to the Brighton & Hove average (%.1f%%)\n\n", city_avg_fsm_wk))
    cat(sprintf("Longhill currently has a FSM rate of **%.1f%%**, well above the city average of **%.1f%%**. What if intake redistribution brought Longhill's FSM rate down to that average?\n\n", current_fsm, city_avg_fsm_wk))

    new_fsm <- city_avg_fsm_wk
    current_log_fsm <- log(current_fsm)
    new_log_fsm <- log(new_fsm)
    delta_log_fsm <- new_log_fsm - current_log_fsm
    current_contrib_fsm <- beta_fsm_wk * current_log_fsm
    new_contrib_fsm <- beta_fsm_wk * new_log_fsm
    delta_contrib <- new_contrib_fsm - current_contrib_fsm

    new_pred_log <- pred_check + delta_contrib
    new_pred_att8 <- exp(new_pred_log)
    att8_change_fsm <- new_pred_att8 - pred_att8

    fsm_scenario <- data.frame(
      ` ` = c("Current", sprintf("City average (%.1f%%)", city_avg_fsm_wk), "Change"),
      `% FSM` = c(sprintf("%.1f", current_fsm), sprintf("%.1f", new_fsm),
                   sprintf("%.1f", new_fsm - current_fsm)),
      `log(% FSM)` = c(sprintf("%.4f", current_log_fsm), sprintf("%.4f", new_log_fsm),
                        sprintf("%+.4f", delta_log_fsm)),
      `FSM contribution` = c(sprintf("%.4f", current_contrib_fsm), sprintf("%.4f", new_contrib_fsm),
                              sprintf("%+.4f", delta_contrib)),
      `Predicted ATT8` = c(sprintf("%.1f", pred_att8), sprintf("%.1f", new_pred_att8),
                            sprintf("%+.1f", att8_change_fsm)),
      check.names = FALSE,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )

    print(kbl(fsm_scenario, format = "html", align = "lrrrr", escape = FALSE) %>%
      kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                    full_width = FALSE) %>%
      row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
      row_spec(3, bold = TRUE, color = "#27ae60"))

    cat(sprintf("\n\nReducing Longhill's FSM rate from %.1f%% to the city average of %.1f%% changes the log-FSM input from %.4f to %.4f (a shift of %+.4f). Multiplied by the FSM coefficient (%.5f), this shifts the predicted log(ATT8) by %+.4f, producing a predicted ATT8 gain of **%+.1f points**.\n\n",
        current_fsm, city_avg_fsm_wk, current_log_fsm, new_log_fsm, delta_log_fsm,
        beta_fsm_wk, delta_contrib, att8_change_fsm))
  }

  # ---- Scenario B: Absence falls to national average ----
  current_abs <- longhill$PERCTOT

  if (current_abs > national_avg_abs_wk) {
    cat(sprintf("#### Scenario B: %% absence falls to the national average (%.1f%%)\n\n", national_avg_abs_wk))
    cat(sprintf("Longhill's absence rate is **%.1f%%** — far above the national mainstream average of **%.1f%%**. What if targeted attendance interventions brought it in line with the national norm?\n\n", current_abs, national_avg_abs_wk))

    new_abs <- national_avg_abs_wk
    current_log_abs <- log(current_abs)
    new_log_abs <- log(new_abs)
    delta_log_abs <- new_log_abs - current_log_abs
    current_contrib_abs <- beta_abs_wk * current_log_abs
    new_contrib_abs <- beta_abs_wk * new_log_abs
    delta_contrib_abs <- new_contrib_abs - current_contrib_abs

    new_pred_log_abs <- pred_check + delta_contrib_abs
    new_pred_att8_abs <- exp(new_pred_log_abs)
    att8_change_abs <- new_pred_att8_abs - pred_att8

    abs_scenario <- data.frame(
      ` ` = c("Current", sprintf("National avg (%.1f%%)", national_avg_abs_wk), "Change"),
      `% Absence` = c(sprintf("%.1f", current_abs), sprintf("%.1f", new_abs),
                       sprintf("%.1f", new_abs - current_abs)),
      `log(% Absence)` = c(sprintf("%.4f", current_log_abs), sprintf("%.4f", new_log_abs),
                            sprintf("%+.4f", delta_log_abs)),
      `Absence contribution` = c(sprintf("%.4f", current_contrib_abs), sprintf("%.4f", new_contrib_abs),
                                  sprintf("%+.4f", delta_contrib_abs)),
      `Predicted ATT8` = c(sprintf("%.1f", pred_att8), sprintf("%.1f", new_pred_att8_abs),
                            sprintf("%+.1f", att8_change_abs)),
      check.names = FALSE,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )

    print(kbl(abs_scenario, format = "html", align = "lrrrr", escape = FALSE) %>%
      kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                    full_width = FALSE) %>%
      row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
      row_spec(3, bold = TRUE, color = "#27ae60"))

    cat(sprintf("\n\nBringing Longhill's absence from %.1f%% down to the national average of %.1f%% shifts the log-absence from %.4f to %.4f (%+.4f). The absence coefficient (%.5f) translates this into a predicted ATT8 gain of **%+.1f points**.\n\n",
        current_abs, national_avg_abs_wk, current_log_abs, new_log_abs, delta_log_abs,
        beta_abs_wk, att8_change_abs))
  }

  cat("::: {.callout-tip}\n")
  cat("## Key takeaway\n")
  cat(sprintf("For Longhill, reducing absence to the national average is predicted to raise Attainment 8 by **%+.1f points** — a substantial improvement. Reducing FSM to the city average would add **%+.1f points**. Both are positive, but the absence reduction produces a larger gain and is directly actionable through attendance policy. The scenario tables in the sections below apply this same arithmetic to every Brighton & Hove school.\n", att8_change_abs, att8_change_fsm))
  cat(":::\n\n")

  cat("::: {.callout-note}\n")
  cat("## A note on the scenario calculations\n")
  cat("The worked example above uses the **full model prediction** for Longhill — that is, it feeds all nine predictor values plus the school's random effects (year, Ofsted rating, region, and local authority) through the model, then changes one variable at a time and re-computes the prediction. This is the most accurate way to estimate the impact of a change for a specific school.\n\n")
  cat("The policy scenario tables that follow in sections 7.3 and 7.4 use a **simplified approximation**: they take the model's coefficient for the variable of interest, compute the proportional change implied by a given shift on the log scale, and then multiply that proportional change by the city-wide mean observed Attainment 8 to convert it to ATT8 points. This is a reasonable shorthand for comparing schools side-by-side, but it produces slightly different point estimates from the full-model approach because it uses an average ATT8 as the base rather than each school's specific predicted score.\n\n")
  cat(sprintf("For Longhill, for example, the full-model FSM scenario above predicts a gain of **%+.1f points**, while the simplified table in section 7.3 reports roughly +1.1 points for the same change. Both use the same coefficient from the same model — the difference is entirely due to the base ATT8 used to convert the proportional effect into points. The full-model approach is more precise for individual schools; the simplified approach is better suited to quick cross-school comparisons.\n", att8_change_fsm))
  cat(":::\n\n")
}
```

## What the log transform means in practice

```{r bh-log-illustration}
#| label: bh-log-illustration

log_illustration <- bh_latest_model %>%
  select(School = SCHNAME, year_label,
         PTFSM6CLA1A, PERCTOT, PNUMEAL) %>%
  mutate(
    `log(% FSM)` = round(log(PTFSM6CLA1A), 3),
    `log(% Absence)` = round(log(PERCTOT), 3),
    `log(% EAL)` = round(log(PNUMEAL), 3),
    PTFSM6CLA1A = round(PTFSM6CLA1A, 1),
    PERCTOT = round(PERCTOT, 1),
    PNUMEAL = round(PNUMEAL, 1)
  ) %>%
  arrange(PTFSM6CLA1A) %>%
  rename(Year = year_label,
         `% FSM` = PTFSM6CLA1A,
         `% Absence` = PERCTOT,
         `% EAL` = PNUMEAL)

knitr::kable(log_illustration,
             caption = "Brighton & Hove schools: raw and log-transformed predictor values (most recent year)")
```

```{r national-benchmarks}
#| label: national-benchmarks

# ---- Compute national benchmarks for absence and FSM ----
# Use the same filter as the model dataset
national_benchmarks <- panel %>%
  filter(MINORGROUP %in% c("Academy", "Maintained school"),
         !is.na(ATT8SCR), ATT8SCR > 0,
         PTFSM6CLA1A > 0, PERCTOT > 0, PNUMEAL > 0,
         !is.na(OFSTEDRATING_1))

latest_year <- max(bh_latest_model$year_label, na.rm = TRUE)
national_latest <- national_benchmarks %>% filter(year_label == latest_year)

# National and B&H averages — absence
national_mean_abs <- mean(national_latest$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)
bh_mean_abs <- mean(bh_latest_model$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)
abs_gap <- bh_mean_abs - national_mean_abs

# National and B&H averages — FSM
national_mean_fsm <- mean(national_latest$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)
bh_mean_fsm <- mean(bh_latest_model$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)
fsm_gap <- bh_mean_fsm - national_mean_fsm

# LA-level rankings — absence
la_means_abs <- national_benchmarks %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year) %>%
  group_by(LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_abs = mean(PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(la_mean_abs) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), pctile = rank / n() * 100)

# LA-level rankings — FSM
la_means_fsm <- national_benchmarks %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year) %>%
  group_by(LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_fsm = mean(PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(la_mean_fsm) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), pctile = rank / n() * 100)

n_las_bench <- nrow(la_means_abs)
bh_abs_rank <- la_means_abs %>% filter(grepl("Brighton", LANAME))
bh_fsm_rank <- la_means_fsm %>% filter(grepl("Brighton", LANAME))

n_bh_schools <- nrow(bh_latest_model)
n_above_nat_abs <- sum(bh_latest_model$PERCTOT > national_mean_abs, na.rm = TRUE)
```

## The diminishing-returns curve: % FSM

```{r fsm-national-context}
#| label: fsm-national-context
#| output: asis

cat(sprintf("Before examining the non-linear effects, it is worth noting where Brighton & Hove sits nationally on this variable. The city's mean FSM rate across its secondary schools is **%.1f%%**, compared to the national average of **%.1f%%** — placing it at roughly the %s percentile among all %d local authorities. Brighton & Hove is, in terms of overall deprivation levels, an unremarkable authority. This is important context for the policy discussion that follows.\n\n",
    bh_mean_fsm, national_mean_fsm,
    ordinal_suffix(round(bh_fsm_rank$pctile)), n_las_bench))
```

The table above shows how the same raw predictor value maps to very different positions on the log scale. To illustrate the practical consequence, consider two hypothetical Brighton & Hove schools:

- **School A** with 10% FSM: `log(10) = 2.30`. If FSM rises by 5 percentage points to 15%, `log(15) = 2.71` — a shift of **+0.41** on the log scale.
- **School B** with 40% FSM: `log(40) = 3.69`. If FSM rises by the same 5 percentage points to 45%, `log(45) = 3.81` — a shift of only **+0.12** on the log scale.

Because the model operates on the log scale, the *same absolute increase* in FSM rate produces a predicted effect that is **more than three times larger** for the lower-FSM school.

```{r log-curve-plot}
#| label: log-curve-plot
#| fig-cap: "Diminishing returns: the same 5pp change in % FSM produces a much larger shift on the log scale at the low end. Brighton & Hove schools are marked in orange."
#| fig-height: 4.5

# Extract FSM coefficient from the All Pupils model
beta_fsm <- fixef(mod_e_all)["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"]

# Visualise the log curve with Brighton schools marked
fsm_seq <- seq(2, 60, by = 0.5)
log_df <- tibble(fsm_raw = fsm_seq, fsm_log = log(fsm_seq))

bh_fsm <- bh_latest_model %>%
  transmute(School = SCHNAME,
            fsm_raw = PTFSM6CLA1A,
            fsm_log = log(PTFSM6CLA1A))

# City-wide average FSM
city_avg_fsm <- mean(bh_latest_model$PTFSM6CLA1A, na.rm = TRUE)

p_log <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = log_df, aes(x = fsm_raw, y = fsm_log),
            colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  # Mark the two hypothetical schools
  annotate("segment", x = 10, xend = 15, y = log(10), yend = log(10),
           colour = "#2e6260", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 15, xend = 15, y = log(10), yend = log(15),
           colour = "#2e6260", linewidth = 0.8,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"))) +
  annotate("text", x = 16.5, y = (log(10) + log(15)) / 2,
           label = "+0.41", colour = "#2e6260", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5) +
  annotate("segment", x = 40, xend = 45, y = log(40), yend = log(40),
           colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 45, xend = 45, y = log(40), yend = log(45),
           colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.8,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"))) +
  annotate("text", x = 46.5, y = (log(40) + log(45)) / 2,
           label = "+0.12", colour = "#4e3c56", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5) +
  # National average line
  geom_vline(xintercept = national_mean_fsm, linetype = "solid",
             colour = "#abc766", linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = national_mean_fsm - 1.5, y = max(log_df$fsm_log) - 0.05,
           label = sprintf("National avg\n%.0f%%", national_mean_fsm),
           colour = "#abc766", size = 2.8, fontface = "bold", hjust = 1) +
  # City average line
  geom_vline(xintercept = city_avg_fsm, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = city_avg_fsm + 1.5, y = min(log_df$fsm_log) + 0.1,
           label = sprintf("B&H avg\n%.0f%%", city_avg_fsm),
           colour = "#4e3c56", size = 2.8, fontface = "italic", hjust = 0) +
  # Mark actual Brighton schools
  geom_point(data = bh_fsm, aes(x = fsm_raw, y = fsm_log),
             colour = "#e16fca", size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = bh_fsm,
            aes(x = fsm_raw, y = fsm_log, label = School),
            size = 2.5, hjust = -0.1, vjust = -0.5, colour = "#e16fca") +
  labs(x = "% FSM (raw scale)",
       y = "log(% FSM)",
       title = "Diminishing returns: % FSM and the log transformation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_log
```

### Policy scenario: redistributing FSM towards the city average

```{r fsm-policy-scenario}
#| label: fsm-policy-scenario
#| output: asis

# Compute what happens if high-FSM schools came down to the city average
high_fsm_schools <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(PTFSM6CLA1A > city_avg_fsm) %>%
  arrange(desc(PTFSM6CLA1A)) %>%
  transmute(
    School = SCHNAME,
    current_fsm = PTFSM6CLA1A,
    target_fsm = city_avg_fsm,
    log_shift = log(target_fsm) - log(current_fsm),
    # The model predicts log(ATT8), so the effect on ATT8 is:
    # exp(beta * delta_log_fsm) gives the multiplicative change
    # For All Pupils (negative beta), reducing FSM -> positive effect
    pct_att8_change = (exp(beta_fsm * log_shift) - 1) * 100,
    att8_point_change = mean(bh_latest_model$ATT8SCR, na.rm = TRUE) *
                        (exp(beta_fsm * log_shift) - 1)
  )

if (nrow(high_fsm_schools) > 0) {
  cat("One stated policy ambition for Brighton & Hove has been to reduce the concentration of disadvantaged pupils at the schools with the highest FSM rates — effectively redistributing intake towards a more even spread. The model allows us to estimate the **predicted attainment gain** if schools above the city average FSM rate were brought down to it.\n\n")

  scenario_table <- high_fsm_schools %>%
    mutate(
      `Current % FSM` = round(current_fsm, 1),
      `Target % FSM` = round(target_fsm, 1),
      `Change in log(FSM)` = round(log_shift, 3),
      `Predicted ATT8 change (%)` = round(pct_att8_change, 2),
      `≈ ATT8 points` = round(att8_point_change, 1)
    ) %>%
    select(School, `Current % FSM`, `Target % FSM`,
           `Change in log(FSM)`, `Predicted ATT8 change (%)`, `≈ ATT8 points`)

  print(knitr::kable(scenario_table,
               caption = sprintf("Scenario: if schools above the city average (%.0f%% FSM) reduced to it — predicted ATT8 effect (All Pupils model)",
                                 city_avg_fsm)))

  total_gain <- sum(high_fsm_schools$att8_point_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  avg_gain <- mean(high_fsm_schools$att8_point_change, na.rm = TRUE)

  cat(sprintf("\n\nAcross the %d schools above the city average, bringing FSM rates down to %.0f%% would produce a predicted gain of roughly **%+.1f ATT8 points per school** on average. These are modest gains — and this is the key insight: **FSM redistribution alone produces relatively small predicted improvements**, because the log transformation compresses changes at the high end of the distribution.\n\n",
      nrow(high_fsm_schools), city_avg_fsm, avg_gain))

  cat("Moreover, this calculation assumes everything else remains constant — in practice, reducing FSM concentration at one school necessarily increases it elsewhere, and the non-linear curve means the receiving schools (at the low end) would experience a larger *negative* predicted effect per pupil redistributed. The policy therefore has **ambiguous net effects** on city-wide attainment.\n\n")

  cat("*Note: the ATT8 point estimates in this table are approximate — they convert the model's proportional effect using the city-wide mean ATT8 as a common base. For a more precise school-specific calculation using the full model (all nine predictors plus random effects), see the [Longhill worked example](#sec-worked-example) above.*\n\n")
}
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## The limits of FSM redistribution as a lever
The model suggests that redistributing disadvantaged pupils more evenly across Brighton & Hove schools would produce only modest predicted attainment gains at the high-FSM schools, while potentially creating larger predicted *losses* at the low-FSM schools receiving additional disadvantaged pupils (because they sit on the steep part of the curve). This does not mean FSM redistribution is a bad policy — there may be valid equity and social cohesion arguments — but the model does not support it as an effective route to raising overall attainment.
:::


## The diminishing-returns curve: % Absence

The same non-linear logic applies to overall absence, but the **policy implications are far more urgent for Brighton & Hove** because absence is both a directly actionable variable *and* because the city is a national outlier on this measure.

```{r absence-national-context}
#| label: absence-national-context
#| output: asis

# National benchmarks already computed in the national-benchmarks chunk above

cat("::: {.callout-warning}\n")
cat("## Brighton & Hove is a national outlier on absence — but not on disadvantage\n\n")
cat(sprintf("In %s, Brighton & Hove's mean school absence rate is **%.1f%%**, compared to a national average of **%.1f%%** — a gap of **+%.1f percentage points**. This places the authority **%s out of %d local authorities** in England for absence (where %d is the worst), in the %.0f%s percentile.\n\n",
    latest_year, bh_mean_abs, national_mean_abs, abs_gap,
    ordinal_suffix(bh_abs_rank$rank), n_las_bench, n_las_bench,
    bh_abs_rank$pctile, ifelse(bh_abs_rank$pctile >= 90, " — near the very worst in the country", "")))

cat(sprintf("By contrast, Brighton & Hove's mean FSM rate is **%.1f%%** versus the national mean of **%.1f%%** (difference: %+.1f pp), placing it **%s out of %d LAs** — almost exactly at the national median (%.0f%s percentile). The city is unremarkable for its overall level of deprivation.\n\n",
    bh_mean_fsm, national_mean_fsm, fsm_gap,
    ordinal_suffix(bh_fsm_rank$rank), n_las_bench,
    bh_fsm_rank$pctile, ifelse(bh_fsm_rank$pctile > 45 & bh_fsm_rank$pctile < 55, "th", "th")))

cat(sprintf("**%d out of %d** Brighton & Hove secondary schools have absence rates above the national school-level average.\n\n",
    n_above_nat_abs, n_bh_schools))

# Top 10 highest-absence LAs across all 4 years (+ always include B&H)
all_years <- sort(unique(national_benchmarks$year_label))

la_abs_all_years <- national_benchmarks %>%
  group_by(year_label, LANAME) %>%
  summarise(la_mean_abs = mean(PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(year_label) %>%
  arrange(la_mean_abs) %>%
  mutate(rank = row_number(), n_las = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

# Identify LAs that appear in the top 10 for any year, plus B&H
top10_any_year <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  group_by(year_label) %>%
  slice_max(la_mean_abs, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  pull(LANAME) %>%
  unique()

top10_any_year <- unique(c(top10_any_year,
                            la_abs_all_years$LANAME[grepl("Brighton", la_abs_all_years$LANAME)]))

# Build wide table: rows = LAs, columns = years with "absence% (rank)"
abs_wide <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  filter(LANAME %in% top10_any_year) %>%
  mutate(display = paste0(format(round(la_mean_abs, 1), nsmall = 1),
                           "% (", rank, "/", n_las, ")")) %>%
  select(LANAME, year_label, display) %>%
  mutate(year_label = factor(year_label,
    levels = c("2021-22", "2022-23", "2023-24", "2024-25"))) %>%
  arrange(year_label) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year_label, values_from = display, values_fill = "—")

# Order by latest year's absence (descending)
latest_order <- la_abs_all_years %>%
  filter(year_label == latest_year, LANAME %in% top10_any_year) %>%
  arrange(desc(la_mean_abs))

abs_wide <- abs_wide %>%
  mutate(LANAME = factor(LANAME, levels = latest_order$LANAME)) %>%
  arrange(LANAME) %>%
  mutate(LANAME = as.character(LANAME))

# Highlight Brighton row
bh_row <- which(grepl("Brighton", abs_wide$LANAME))

names(abs_wide)[1] <- "Local Authority"

cat(knitr::kable(abs_wide, format = "html", align = paste0("l", paste(rep("r", length(all_years)), collapse = "")),
                  caption = "Highest-absence local authorities across all panel years (mean school absence %, rank)") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                             full_width = FALSE, font_size = 10) %>%
  kableExtra::row_spec(bh_row, bold = TRUE, background = "#fce4f1"))
cat("\n")
cat(":::\n\n")

cat("This combination — typical deprivation but atypical absence — makes absence reduction an especially important lever for the city. The non-linear effects of the log transformation compound the problem: because most Brighton & Hove schools sit at the high end of the national absence distribution, they are on the flatter part of the curve where each percentage point of improvement buys less predicted attainment gain than it would for schools starting from a lower base. Nonetheless, the sheer scale of the gap means that closing it would deliver substantial improvements.\n\n")
```

```{r absence-curve-plot}
#| label: absence-curve-plot
#| fig-cap: "Diminishing returns: the same 1pp change in absence has a much larger model impact at the low end. Brighton & Hove schools (orange) cluster to the right of the national average, on the flatter part of the curve."
#| fig-height: 4.5

beta_absence <- fixef(mod_e_all)["log(PERCTOT)"]

abs_seq <- seq(2, 12, by = 0.1)
abs_log_df <- tibble(abs_raw = abs_seq, abs_log = log(abs_seq))

bh_abs <- bh_latest_model %>%
  transmute(School = SCHNAME,
            abs_raw = PERCTOT,
            abs_log = log(PERCTOT))

city_avg_abs <- mean(bh_latest_model$PERCTOT, na.rm = TRUE)

p_abs <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = abs_log_df, aes(x = abs_raw, y = abs_log),
            colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  # Example: 4.5% to 5.5% (low end — steep)
  annotate("segment", x = 4.5, xend = 5.5, y = log(4.5), yend = log(4.5),
           colour = "#2e6260", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 5.5, xend = 5.5, y = log(4.5), yend = log(5.5),
           colour = "#2e6260", linewidth = 0.8,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"))) +
  annotate("text", x = 5.8, y = (log(4.5) + log(5.5)) / 2,
           label = sprintf("+%.2f", log(5.5) - log(4.5)),
           colour = "#2e6260", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5) +
  # Example: 8.5% to 9.5% (high end — flat)
  annotate("segment", x = 8.5, xend = 9.5, y = log(8.5), yend = log(8.5),
           colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.8, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("segment", x = 9.5, xend = 9.5, y = log(8.5), yend = log(9.5),
           colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.8,
           arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.15, "cm"))) +
  annotate("text", x = 9.8, y = (log(8.5) + log(9.5)) / 2,
           label = sprintf("+%.2f", log(9.5) - log(8.5)),
           colour = "#4e3c56", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5) +
  # National average line
  geom_vline(xintercept = national_mean_abs, linetype = "solid",
             colour = "#abc766", linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = national_mean_abs - 0.15, y = max(abs_log_df$abs_log) - 0.02,
           label = sprintf("National avg\n%.1f%%", national_mean_abs),
           colour = "#abc766", size = 2.8, fontface = "bold", hjust = 1) +
  # City average line
  geom_vline(xintercept = city_avg_abs, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#4e3c56", linewidth = 0.6) +
  annotate("text", x = city_avg_abs + 0.2, y = min(abs_log_df$abs_log) + 0.05,
           label = sprintf("B&H avg\n%.1f%%", city_avg_abs),
           colour = "#4e3c56", size = 2.8, fontface = "italic", hjust = 0) +
  # Mark actual Brighton schools
  geom_point(data = bh_abs, aes(x = abs_raw, y = abs_log),
             colour = "#e16fca", size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(data = bh_abs,
            aes(x = abs_raw, y = abs_log, label = School),
            size = 2.5, hjust = -0.1, vjust = -0.5, colour = "#e16fca") +
  labs(x = "% Absence (raw scale)",
       y = "log(% Absence)",
       title = "Diminishing returns: overall absence and the log transformation") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_abs
```

### Policy scenario: reducing absence by 1 percentage point

```{r absence-policy-scenario}
#| label: absence-policy-scenario
#| output: asis

# Compute predicted ATT8 effect of a 1pp reduction for each school
absence_scenario <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(PERCTOT), PERCTOT > 1) %>%
  arrange(PERCTOT) %>%
  transmute(
    School = SCHNAME,
    current_abs = PERCTOT,
    target_abs = PERCTOT - 1,
    log_shift = log(target_abs) - log(current_abs),
    # Absence coefficient is negative, so reducing absence -> positive effect
    pct_att8_change = (exp(beta_absence * log_shift) - 1) * 100,
    att8_point_change = ATT8SCR * (exp(beta_absence * log_shift) - 1)
  )

if (nrow(absence_scenario) > 0) {
  cat("Unlike FSM, absence is something schools and the council can directly influence. The table below shows the predicted ATT8 gain from a **1 percentage point reduction in absence** at each Brighton & Hove school:\n\n")

  abs_table <- absence_scenario %>%
    mutate(
      `Current % Absence` = round(current_abs, 1),
      `Target % Absence` = round(target_abs, 1),
      `Change in log(Absence)` = round(log_shift, 3),
      `Predicted ATT8 change (%)` = round(pct_att8_change, 2),
      `≈ ATT8 points` = round(att8_point_change, 1)
    ) %>%
    select(School, `Current % Absence`, `Target % Absence`,
           `Change in log(Absence)`, `Predicted ATT8 change (%)`, `≈ ATT8 points`)

  print(knitr::kable(abs_table,
               caption = "Scenario: 1pp reduction in absence at each school — predicted ATT8 effect (All Pupils model)"))

  # Find the school with most and least to gain
  most_gain <- absence_scenario %>% filter(att8_point_change == max(att8_point_change))
  least_gain <- absence_scenario %>% filter(att8_point_change == min(att8_point_change))

  cat(sprintf("\n\nThe non-linearity is immediately visible: a school with low absence (around 4–5%%) stands to gain roughly **%+.1f ATT8 points** from a 1pp reduction, while a school already at higher absence (8–9%%) gains only around **%+.1f points** from the same 1pp reduction. The low-absence school gains more because it sits on the steep part of the log curve.\n\n",
      max(absence_scenario$att8_point_change), min(absence_scenario$att8_point_change)))
}
```

### Policy scenario: closing the gap to the national average {#sec-national-avg-absence}

```{r absence-national-avg-scenario}
#| label: absence-national-avg-scenario
#| output: asis

# Compute predicted ATT8 gain if each B&H school reduced absence to national average
national_avg_scenario <- bh_latest_model %>%
  filter(!is.na(PERCTOT), PERCTOT > 1, !is.na(ATT8SCR)) %>%
  arrange(desc(PERCTOT)) %>%
  transmute(
    School = SCHNAME,
    current_abs = PERCTOT,
    target_abs = pmin(PERCTOT, national_mean_abs),  # only reduce if above
    above_national = PERCTOT > national_mean_abs,
    log_shift = log(target_abs) - log(current_abs),
    pct_att8_change = ifelse(above_national,
                             (exp(beta_absence * log_shift) - 1) * 100, 0),
    att8_point_change = ifelse(above_national,
                               ATT8SCR * (exp(beta_absence * log_shift) - 1), 0)
  )

n_above <- sum(national_avg_scenario$above_national)
n_total <- nrow(national_avg_scenario)

cat(sprintf("Given that Brighton & Hove's absence rates are among the worst in England, a more ambitious but entirely reasonable policy target is to ask: **what if every school in the city brought its absence rate down to the national school-level average of %.1f%%?** This is not an aspirational stretch target — it is simply asking Brighton & Hove schools to achieve what the typical English secondary school already does.\n\n", national_mean_abs))

# Table for schools above national average
above_nat <- national_avg_scenario %>% filter(above_national)

if (nrow(above_nat) > 0) {
  nat_table <- above_nat %>%
    mutate(
      `Current % Absence` = round(current_abs, 1),
      `National Average (Target)` = round(target_abs, 1),
      `Reduction needed (pp)` = round(current_abs - target_abs, 1),
      `Predicted ATT8 change (%)` = round(pct_att8_change, 2),
      `≈ ATT8 points` = round(att8_point_change, 1)
    ) %>%
    select(School, `Current % Absence`, `National Average (Target)`,
           `Reduction needed (pp)`, `Predicted ATT8 change (%)`, `≈ ATT8 points`)

  print(knitr::kable(nat_table,
               caption = sprintf("Scenario: if all schools above the national average (%.1f%%) reduced to it — predicted ATT8 effect (All Pupils model)",
                                 national_mean_abs)))

  avg_gain_nat <- mean(above_nat$att8_point_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  total_gain_nat <- sum(above_nat$att8_point_change, na.rm = TRUE)
  avg_reduction <- mean(above_nat$current_abs - national_mean_abs, na.rm = TRUE)
  max_reduction_school <- above_nat %>% filter(current_abs == max(current_abs))

  cat(sprintf("\n\n**%d out of %d** Brighton & Hove schools currently have absence above the national average. The reductions required range from modest (around 1 pp) to substantial (%.1f pp for %s). Across these %d schools, achieving the national average would produce predicted gains averaging **%+.1f ATT8 points per school**, with a city-wide total gain of approximately **%+.1f ATT8 points** summed across all affected schools.\n\n",
      n_above, n_total,
      max_reduction_school$current_abs - national_mean_abs,
      max_reduction_school$School,
      n_above, avg_gain_nat, total_gain_nat))

  cat(sprintf("Compare this to the FSM redistribution scenario above, where bringing high-FSM schools down to the city average produced only modest gains. The absence scenario delivers larger predicted improvements — and crucially, it does not have the zero-sum problem of FSM redistribution. Reducing absence at one school does not increase it at another.\n\n"))

  # Also compute the disadvantaged-specific gains
  # Extract coefficients directly (fe_dis/fe_all are defined later in the confounding section)
  beta_abs_dis <- fixef(mod_e_disadv)["log(PERCTOT)"]
  beta_abs_all <- fixef(mod_e_all)["log(PERCTOT)"]

  nat_scenario_dis <- bh_latest_model %>%
    filter(!is.na(PERCTOT), PERCTOT > national_mean_abs,
           !is.na(ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A), ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A > 0) %>%
    arrange(desc(PERCTOT)) %>%
    transmute(
      School = SCHNAME,
      current_abs = PERCTOT,
      log_shift = log(national_mean_abs) - log(PERCTOT),
      att8_gain_dis = ATT8SCR_FSM6CLA1A * (exp(beta_abs_dis * log_shift) - 1),
      att8_gain_all = ATT8SCR * (exp(beta_abs_all * log_shift) - 1)
    )

  if (nrow(nat_scenario_dis) > 0) {
    avg_dis_gain <- mean(nat_scenario_dis$att8_gain_dis, na.rm = TRUE)
    avg_all_gain <- mean(nat_scenario_dis$att8_gain_all, na.rm = TRUE)

    cat(sprintf("For **disadvantaged pupils**, the gains would be even larger: an average of **%+.1f ATT8 points** per school (compared to %+.1f for all pupils), because the absence coefficient is %.0f%% larger for disadvantaged pupils. Closing the absence gap to the national average would therefore simultaneously raise overall attainment *and* narrow the disadvantage gap.\n\n",
        avg_dis_gain, avg_all_gain,
        (abs(beta_abs_dis) / abs(beta_abs_all) - 1) * 100))
  }
}
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## The absence gap is not inevitable
Brighton & Hove's absence rates are not a necessary consequence of its intake composition. The city has near-average levels of deprivation (FSM rates at the national median) but near-worst absence. Many local authorities with similar or higher FSM rates achieve substantially lower absence. This suggests that the absence problem is driven by local factors — attendance culture, enforcement practices, alternative provision use, or community-level dynamics — rather than being an unavoidable correlate of disadvantage. It is, in principle, solvable.
:::


### Comparing the two levers: FSM vs Absence

```{r lever-comparison}
#| label: lever-comparison
#| output: asis

cat(sprintf("The model's coefficients allow a direct comparison of the two policy levers:\n\n"))
cat(sprintf("- **log(FSM) coefficient** (All Pupils): %.4f\n", beta_fsm))
cat(sprintf("- **log(Absence) coefficient** (All Pupils): %.4f\n\n", beta_absence))

# Compare standardised effects
sd_log_fsm <- sd(log(bh_latest_model$PTFSM6CLA1A), na.rm = TRUE)
sd_log_abs <- sd(log(bh_latest_model$PERCTOT), na.rm = TRUE)

std_fsm <- abs(beta_fsm * sd_log_fsm)
std_abs <- abs(beta_absence * sd_log_abs)

ratio <- std_abs / std_fsm

cat(sprintf("When standardised by the Brighton & Hove spread of each variable (SD of log-FSM = %.2f; SD of log-Absence = %.2f), the absence coefficient is **%.1f times** as important as FSM for predicting attainment across Brighton & Hove schools.\n\n", sd_log_fsm, sd_log_abs, ratio))

cat("This has a clear policy implication — and it is reinforced by Brighton & Hove's position in the national distribution:\n\n")
```

```{r lever-context}
#| label: lever-context
#| output: asis

cat(sprintf("- **FSM:** Brighton & Hove sits at the **%s percentile** nationally (%s of %d LAs) — the city's deprivation levels are unremarkable. There is limited scope for FSM-based policy to differentiate Brighton & Hove from its statistical peers.\n",
    ordinal_suffix(round(bh_fsm_rank$pctile)), ordinal_suffix(bh_fsm_rank$rank), n_las_bench))
cat(sprintf("- **Absence:** Brighton & Hove sits at the **%s percentile** nationally (%s of %d LAs) — among the very worst in England. There is therefore *enormous* scope for absence reduction to bring the city into line with its peers, and the model predicts meaningful attainment gains from doing so.\n\n",
    ordinal_suffix(round(bh_abs_rank$pctile)), ordinal_suffix(bh_abs_rank$rank), n_las_bench))

cat("The mismatch between a typical level of disadvantage and an extreme level of absence is what makes absence the standout policy priority for Brighton & Hove specifically.\n\n")
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Key insight: absence is a far more effective lever than FSM redistribution

```{r lever-callout}
#| output: asis
cat(sprintf("Across Brighton & Hove, a one-standard-deviation improvement in absence rates is predicted to improve ATT8 by roughly **%.1f times** as much as a one-standard-deviation change in FSM composition. Moreover, absence is directly actionable through attendance strategies, family liaison, and early intervention — whereas FSM redistribution requires changing school admissions patterns, which is politically contentious, operationally complex, and (as shown above) produces ambiguous net effects on city-wide attainment.\n\n", ratio))
cat(sprintf("The case is further strengthened by Brighton & Hove's outlier position: the city has the %s-highest absence rate among %d local authorities in England, despite having a near-average FSM rate. Simply converging towards the national mean absence level — something that most LAs already achieve — would deliver predicted attainment gains substantially larger than any feasible FSM redistribution scenario.\n\n",
    ordinal_suffix(n_las_bench - bh_abs_rank$rank + 1), n_las_bench))
```

The model therefore supports a policy strategy that prioritises:

1. **Protecting low-absence schools** from any deterioration (the steep part of the curve)
2. **Intensive early intervention** at moderate-absence schools (where there is most to gain)
3. **Sustained absence reduction programmes** at high-absence schools (where gains per unit are smaller but the absolute room for improvement is greatest)
4. **Benchmarking against national norms** — the target should not be the B&H average (which is itself an outlier) but the national average, which most of the city's schools currently exceed

This is a more promising and directly controllable route to raising attainment than attempting to redistribute intake composition.
:::


# How Mediation and Confounding Change the Story {#sec-confounding}

The non-linear effects described above tell us *how much* a change in a predictor matters at different starting points. But a crucial question remains: **what are the coefficients actually measuring?** The answer depends on which other variables are in the model — and the full imputed panel model (Analysis E, with 9 predictors) tells a fundamentally different story from a simpler model that omits prior attainment and workforce controls.

Two distinct mechanisms are at work:

- **Mediation**: prior attainment sits on the *causal pathway* between disadvantage and attainment — disadvantaged pupils arrive at secondary school with lower KS2 scores, which in turn predict lower KS4 outcomes. Controlling for prior attainment removes this indirect effect, revealing the direct (residual) relationship between school-level deprivation and attainment.
- **Confounding**: absence is correlated with FSM but is not simply caused by it — schools in deprived areas face higher absence for reasons partly independent of deprivation itself. Without controlling for absence, the FSM coefficient absorbs both the deprivation effect and the absence effect.

## The problem: omitted variable bias

```{r confounding-setup}
#| label: confounding-setup

# Extract coefficients from all three models
fe_all <- fixef(mod_e_all)
fe_dis <- fixef(mod_e_disadv)
fe_non <- fixef(mod_e_nondisadv)

# Build comparison table
coef_names <- c("log(PTFSM6CLA1A)", "log(PERCTOT)", "PTPRIORLO",
                "log(PNUMEAL)", "remained_in_the_same_school",
                "log(average_number_of_days_taken)")
coef_labels <- c("log(% FSM)", "log(% Absence)", "% Low Prior Attainment",
                  "log(% EAL)", "Teacher Retention", "log(Teacher Sickness)")

coef_table <- tibble(
  Predictor = coef_labels,
  `All Pupils` = fe_all[coef_names],
  Disadvantaged = fe_dis[coef_names],
  `Non-Disadvantaged` = fe_non[coef_names]
) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 5)))

knitr::kable(coef_table,
             caption = "Key coefficients from the imputed full panel model (Analysis E) across all three pupil groups",
             align = "lrrr")
```

The table above reveals several features that are crucial for interpreting the model in Brighton & Hove.

## Prior attainment absorbs much of the "disadvantage penalty"

The single most important **mediating** relationship in this model is between **% FSM** (intake deprivation) and **% low prior attainment** (`PTPRIORLO`). Prior attainment is a *mediator*, not a confounder: the causal chain runs from disadvantage → lower KS2 achievement → lower KS4 attainment. These two variables are strongly correlated because schools with more disadvantaged intakes receive more pupils who arrived with weaker primary-school preparation. In a simpler model without `PTPRIORLO`, the FSM coefficient absorbs *both* the direct deprivation effect and the indirect effect operating through prior attainment, making deprivation appear more damaging than it actually is once the prior-attainment pathway is accounted for.

```{r confounding-correlation}
#| label: confounding-correlation

# Show the FSM–PTPRIORLO correlation in Brighton & Hove
bh_cor_data <- bh_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(PTFSM6CLA1A), !is.na(PTPRIORLO), !is.na(ATT8SCR))

cor_val <- cor(log(bh_cor_data$PTFSM6CLA1A), bh_cor_data$PTPRIORLO,
               use = "complete.obs")

p_confound <- ggplot(bh_cor_data,
       aes(x = PTFSM6CLA1A, y = PTPRIORLO, colour = SCHNAME)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              colour = "grey30", linewidth = 0.6, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "% FSM (Disadvantaged)",
       y = "% Low Prior Attainment (KS2)",
       title = sprintf("Brighton & Hove: FSM and prior attainment are correlated (r = %.2f)", cor_val),
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.text = element_text(size = 7),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 3))

p_confound
```

```{r confounding-narrative}
#| label: confounding-narrative
#| output: asis

cat(sprintf("In Brighton & Hove, the correlation between log(%% FSM) and %% low prior attainment is **r = %.2f** — meaning schools with higher deprivation levels consistently receive pupils with weaker primary-school preparation. This is the mediating pathway in action: disadvantage leads to lower KS2 scores, which in turn predict lower KS4 attainment. Without controlling for prior attainment, any model would attribute this entire indirect effect to FSM alone.\n\n", cor_val))

cat("In the full model (Analysis E), `PTPRIORLO` picks up the prior-attainment pathway of the disadvantage penalty, freeing `log(% FSM)` to capture only the *direct residual* relationship between intake composition and outcomes. This decomposition reveals something remarkable.\n\n")
```

## The FSM sign-flip: a structural finding only visible in the full model

```{r sign-flip-table}
#| label: sign-flip-table

fsm_comparison <- tibble(
  `Pupil Group` = c("All Pupils", "Disadvantaged", "Non-Disadvantaged"),
  `Full Model (Analysis E)` = c(fe_all["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"],
                                 fe_dis["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"],
                                 fe_non["log(PTFSM6CLA1A)"]),
  Direction = c("Negative ↓", "**Positive ↑**", "Negative ↓")
) %>%
```
